program: Travel English -- Europe Without a Translator
hub_subtitle: Travel English for the airport, the check-in counter, the hotel and passport control — so that Europe needs no translator

lesson.menu_title: On Her Own -- The Whole Journey, Gate to Door
lesson.menu_desc: The full simulation: one travel day from the plane to the hotel, alone, with every tense of the program working together. Present perfect for what has just happened, past simple for the finished steps, present continuous for right now, going to for the next move -- landing, passport control, the bag, the train, the door. New words for handling it yourself: on my own, to handle, to sort out, to go through, to keep track of, to double-check, right away, smooth -- 34 slides
lesson.subtitle: Lesson 15 -- On Her Own
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Rosângela | Lesson 15 -- On Her Own
phases: From the Gate to the Door

listenings.text: Good afternoon, and welcome to Lisbon. My name is Bruno, and I work here in the arrivals hall, so let me walk you through the next hour, because a lot of you are traveling alone for the first time, and the airport is big, and you are tired. First, the good news: the hard part is behind you. Your plane has already landed. You have already done the eleven hours. What is left is small, and it is all in one line. So here is the line. One: you have just come off the plane, so follow the purple signs to passport control. Have your passport open and ready in your hand before you get there. Two: after passport control, go down to baggage, and find your belt number on the screen. If your bag has not come out yet, do not panic. Wait. It always comes. Three: when you have your bag, walk straight out through the green channel, unless you have something to declare. Four, and this is the one people get wrong: do not take the first taxi that shouts at you. Keep track of your money. The train and the metro are cheaper, they are clean, and they go straight to the city. Now, one more thing, and then I will let you go. Some of you are worried because you are on your own, and there is nobody to translate. Listen to me. You do not need perfect English to get from this hall to your hotel. You need six words: passport, bag, train, hotel, please, and thank you. That is the whole journey. You have already flown across an ocean by yourself. You can handle the last hour. Follow the signs, keep your passport close, and welcome to Portugal.

My name is Inês, I am seventy-one, and I want to tell you about the first time I did a whole trip on my own, because for forty years I never did. My husband did everything. He checked us in, he talked at passport control, he found the taxi, he asked for the room. I just followed him, with my hands folded, like a good quiet wife, and I saw the world through the back of his coat. And then he was gone, and my daughter said, Mother, you cannot travel now, you do not speak English, you will get lost. And something in me stood up. So I booked a flight to Porto, alone, and I did not tell her until the morning I left. I will be honest with you: at the airport in São Paulo my hands were shaking. But I had made a little card, and on the card I had written every step in order. Check in. Go through security. Find the gate. Board. And I did them, one at a time, like climbing stairs, and I did not look at the whole staircase, only the next step. In Porto I went through passport control, I collected my bag, I found the train, and I checked into my hotel, and the young man at the desk had no idea that he was the first person in a foreign country I had ever spoken to without my husband beside me. When I closed the door of that room, I sat on the bed, and I cried, and then I laughed, because the trip had been completely smooth, and the only hard part, the whole time, had been believing I could. So if you are a woman my age, and everybody is telling you what you cannot do, let me tell you what I know. You have handled harder things than an airport. Make your little card. Take the next step. And go.

structure.title: One journey, four tenses, working together
cards: For what has just happened or is already done, when the moment is still fresh: "I have just landed." "I have already gone through passport control." "I haven't collected my bag yet." This is the news of right now.
checked, took, was -- with a closed time

For a step that finished at a set time: "I checked in in São Paulo this morning." "The flight took off at ten." If you can say WHEN (this morning, at ten, yesterday), use the past simple, never the present perfect.
For what is happening this very second, in front of you: "I am waiting at the gate." "I am looking for the train." "They are boarding now." It is the thing you can see as you speak.
am going to + verb (and I'll for a quick decision)
For the plan and the next move: "I am going to take the train to the city." "I'll ask at the information desk." Going to for the plan you already have; I'll for a decision you make on the spot.

items.situation: You have just come off the plane in Lisbon after eleven hours. A ground agent asks how the flight was. Tell him you have just arrived, and how you feel.
tips: I have just landed, and I am exhausted, but I am fine.
I have just arrived. It was a long flight, but a smooth one.

items.situation: He asks if you checked in for this flight. You did it in São Paulo, early this morning. That is a finished time -- choose the right tense.
tips: Yes, I checked in in São Paulo this morning. (finished time, so past simple)
I checked in early this morning, before the flight.

items.situation: He asks if you have been through passport control. You have -- it is done. Use the good-news word.
tips: Yes, I have already gone through passport control.
I have already gone through. My passport is stamped.

items.situation: You are at the baggage belt, but your suitcase is still not there. Tell him, using the word that waits at the end.
tips: My bag hasn't come out yet.
I haven't collected my suitcase yet -- I am still waiting.

items.situation: He asks what you are doing right now, at this second. You are looking for the train. Use the tense for right now.
tips: I am looking for the train to the city.
Right now I am trying to find the platform for the train.

items.situation: He asks how you are getting to the hotel. It is your plan -- the train, and then a short walk. Tell him.
tips: I am going to take the train, and then walk to the hotel.
I'm going to take the metro to the city, and walk from there.

items.situation: Your phone has no signal, and you need internet to find the hotel. You want to fix this small problem now. Say it with today's words.
tips: I need to sort out a SIM card right away.
I have to sort out my phone first -- I'll buy a SIM card here.

items.situation: He smiles and says you are doing all of this alone. Tell him you are on your own this time, and that you can handle it.
tips: Yes, I am on my own this time, and I can handle it.
My husband is not here -- I'm on my own, but I can handle the airport now.
